title: Agentic Support Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for customer-support / CX agents that act in real time — answering questions and taking transactional actions (refund, cancel, reschedule) on the customer's behalf, mid-conversation, and handing off to a human when needed. It introduces an axis the rest of the series doesn't have: every other vertical gates BEFORE an async action, but support gates INSIDE a live loop under a turn-taking latency budget. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds two vertical-specific things: a product/policy-KB-plus-scoped-account grounding corpus, and an in-loop action gate that classifies each action by reversibility and decides auto-execute vs approve vs escalate — with transactional side effects made safe by idempotency and rollback.

nodes.label: Customer
nodes.description: The person in a live conversation; the agent acts on their behalf and hands off when needed.

nodes.label: Human Agent
nodes.description: Support agent who takes escalations and approves high-risk actions, with conversation context.

nodes.label: Live Channel
nodes.description: Chat, voice, or messaging surface where the real-time conversation happens.

nodes.label: Support API
nodes.description: Authenticates the session, resolves the customer's scope, routes to the conversation loop.

nodes.label: Conversation Agent
nodes.description: Drives the real-time turn loop: understand, retrieve, decide deflect/act/escalate.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared low-latency LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Product / Policy KB
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: product docs, help articles, and refund/return policy.

nodes.label: Account / Order Record
nodes.description: The customer's account, orders, and entitlements; the read and action surface (scoped).

nodes.label: In-Loop Action Gate
nodes.description: Classifies an action by reversibility/risk and decides auto-execute, approve, or escalate — inside the live turn.

nodes.label: Transaction Engine
nodes.description: Executes refunds, cancels, reschedules with provider-scoped idempotency, reconciliation for ambiguous outcomes, and compensating actions where reversal is possible.

nodes.label: Payments / Commerce
nodes.description: Payment and order systems for refunds and (optionally) agentic checkout.

nodes.label: Escalation Router
nodes.description: Routes deflect-vs-escalate and hands a conversation to a human with full context.

nodes.label: Session / Durable State
nodes.description: Live conversation state plus a durable log so an action resumes without duplicating a committed side effect.

nodes.label: Policy & Safety
nodes.description: Entitlement/policy checks, PII handling, and screening of untrusted customer input.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated authority to act on the customer's behalf; ties approval to a human agent.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: turns, retrieved facts, action decisions, approvals, transactions, handoffs.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, latency/CSAT/resolution metrics, deflection and action-correctness eval.

functional: Hold a real-time conversation across chat/voice/messaging channels.
Answer grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped account/order record.
Take transactional actions (refund, cancel, reschedule) on the customer's behalf within the conversation.
Decide, per turn, whether to deflect (answer), act, or escalate to a human.
Hand off to a human agent with full conversation context when needed.
nonFunctional: Real-time: each turn must respond within a latency budget; the gate runs inside the loop, not before it.
Transactional side effects use provider-scoped idempotency (effectively-once with reconciliation); reversal is by compensation where possible, manual repair where not.
Higher-risk / less-reversible actions require human approval; low-risk reversible ones can auto-execute.
Act only with scoped, delegated authority; customer input is untrusted.
Auditable: every turn, action decision, approval, transaction, and handoff is recorded.

capacity.label: Active load
capacity.note: ~2 active turns/min/convo -> ~1.7k turns/sec peak; channel split skews chat-heavy with a smaller voice tail that needs partial responses.

capacity.label: Action mix
capacity.note: Of turns that propose actions: ~70% low-risk auto-execute, ~20% need human approval, ~10% escalate. Approval adds a human queue with its own SLA.

capacity.label: Retrieval fan-out
capacity.note: ~1 policy/KB search plus 1-2 account/order reads per actionable turn, run in parallel under the turn deadline.

capacity.label: Turn latency
capacity.note: Text turn p95 < 2.5s, stream first token < 800ms; voice uses partial verbal responses and async action status.

capacity.label: Provider limits
capacity.note: Commerce/payment/account systems have stricter QPS, retries, and circuit breakers than the chat frontend; back-pressure and timeouts protect them.

capacity.label: Audit volume
capacity.note: Turn + retrieval + gate + transaction + handoff events; retention 1-7 years depending on tenant policy.

capacity.label: Side effects
capacity.note: Provider-scoped idempotency keys dedupe retries; ambiguous timeouts reconcile via provider lookup/webhook, with a manual-repair queue for unknown outcomes.

api.description: Process one live turn: understand, retrieve, and decide deflect/act/escalate within the latency budget. Fields are illustrative of the controls a safe live turn needs, not an exhaustive contract.
A human agent approves or rejects a high-risk action proposed mid-conversation; only an approval mints a scoped action token and executes. The proposed action expires if not decided in time.
Hand the conversation to a human agent with a full context package: transcript pointer, summary, attempted actions, and an account snapshot.

dataModel.name: conversation_turns
dataModel.note: One turn of the live loop; durable so a turn resumes after a restart.

dataModel.name: action_proposals
dataModel.note: An action the agent proposes inside a turn, before it is executed.

dataModel.name: approval_decisions
dataModel.note: A human agent's decision on a proposed action, bound to a policy snapshot.

dataModel.name: transaction_attempts
dataModel.note: One attempt to execute an action against a provider; idempotency is per attempt key.

dataModel.name: compensations
dataModel.note: A compensating action that reverses a committed side effect.

dataModel.name: tenant_action_policies
dataModel.note: Per-tenant rule for an action kind: who can do it, up to what limit, and how it must be authorized. The gate reads the active version per action.

dataModel.name: delegated_token_grants
dataModel.note: A scoped, action-bound on-behalf-of token Identity mints only after gate + approval pass; can't be reused for a different action.

dataModel.name: verification_challenges
dataModel.note: Customer identity-proofing / consent for an action, when tenant policy requires proofing before a scoped action.

dataModel.name: handoffs
dataModel.note: A human-handoff package and its routing/SLA state.

dataModel.name: knowledge_sources
dataModel.note: Retrieval evidence captured per turn for grounding and audit.

dataModel.name: audit_record
dataModel.note: Append-only, hash-chained conversation audit with retention policy.

patterns.name: Agentic loop (ReAct)
patterns.what: A real-time understand -> retrieve -> decide -> respond loop per conversational turn.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: High-risk actions need a human agent's approval mid-conversation.
patterns.whenToUse: Before a hard-to-reverse action on the customer's account.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent converses and acts freely but a reversibility-tiered gate fences the action.
patterns.whenToUse: Live action-taking with a risk boundary inside the loop.

patterns.name: Durable execution / checkpointing (resume after crash)
patterns.what: Provider-scoped idempotency with reconciliation and compensating actions so a mid-action failure resumes effectively-once (manual repair for unknown outcomes).

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped, delegated authority to act on the customer's behalf; approval tied to a human agent.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground replies in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped record.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat customer messages as untrusted; partition from account-action authority.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate resolution quality and action correctness, not just CSAT.

technologyChoices.concern: Channel, contact-center, and realtime messaging integration
tradeoff: Self-hosted channel stacks give tight control over streaming, media, and transcript handling, but the team owns carrier/chat reliability and agent-presence integration.
Managed contact-center platforms ship omnichannel routing, recording, and agent desktop workflows, but they constrain how deeply the live agent loop can customize latency and handoff behavior.
For chat-first products, a lightweight WebSocket or server-sent-events edge can be enough; voice needs a dedicated media/control-plane integration.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed contact-center platform removes most custom channel gateway, recording, presence, and agent-desktop work, but not the in-loop action gate.

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, tenant auth, and turn admission control
tradeoff: The edge must resolve tenant and customer scope before retrieval or action proposals, then shed load before the model queue destroys the p95 turn budget.
A self-hosted gateway gives portable policy and custom admission logic; managed API edges reduce operations and add WAF/rate-limit primitives.
Keep per-tenant budgets at the edge because downstream commerce and payment systems usually have stricter QPS limits than the conversation channel.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with WAF, quota, and WebSocket support removes much custom ingress plumbing, but it still needs application-level customer-scope enforcement.

technologyChoices.concern: Conversation turn state and low-latency session store
tradeoff: Durable turn records make the live loop resumable after process restarts and provide the state needed for handoff packages.
Relational storage is easiest for conversation, proposal, approval, and handoff joins; a KV/wide-column store handles high write volume and TTL-heavy session cursors better.
A cache is useful for hot turn context, but it should not be the only copy of action proposals, approvals, or transaction attempts.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed database with TTL, conditional writes, and global replication can remove custom sharding and cleanup jobs for live session state.

technologyChoices.concern: Policy / product KB and hybrid retrieval
tradeoff: Support grounding needs both semantic policy retrieval and exact account/order facts; mixing vector, keyword, and structured reads is more reliable than a pure vector store.
Self-hosted search keeps corpus control and cost predictable, but the team owns ingestion, freshness, hybrid ranking, and reindexing.
Managed KB/retrieval services reduce ingestion and embedding operations, while making ranking, chunking, and tenant-isolation behavior more provider-specific.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed KB with ingestion, embeddings, hybrid search, and versioned document metadata removes much of the custom chunking and index-refresh pipeline.

technologyChoices.concern: LLM serving, model gateway, and prompt cache
tradeoff: The live loop needs first-token streaming, strict token budgets, provider failover, and per-tenant model policy; a gateway is the right place to centralize those controls.
Self-hosted inference can lower cost and keep transcripts in the team's boundary, but it shifts GPU capacity, batching, evals, and model upgrades in-house.
Managed frontier-model providers reduce serving operations, but the support platform still owns prompt-injection isolation, grounding evidence, and action authority boundaries.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A mature model gateway with caching, routing, redaction, and audit logging removes repeated provider-client code in every agent component.

technologyChoices.concern: Safety guardrails, PII handling, and prompt-injection screening
tradeoff: Guardrails should reduce unsafe replies and data exposure, but they must not become the authority source for account actions; the deterministic gate still decides side effects.
Self-hosted redaction and injection checks keep sensitive transcripts local, while managed services reduce classifier maintenance and policy updates.
Screening needs to happen on customer text, retrieved snippets, tool outputs, and generated action payloads because any of them can carry untrusted instructions.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider safety and PII services can remove some custom classifier work, but not the architectural separation between untrusted conversation content and privileged action authority.

technologyChoices.concern: Action gate, policy engine, and risk-tier rules
tradeoff: A dedicated policy engine makes reversibility, thresholds, proofing, and approval rules versioned and testable instead of scattered through prompt text or service code.
Cloud authorization services integrate with IAM but often model resource permissions better than support-specific action tiers, so business risk logic may still live in OPA/Cedar/Drools.
Learned risk scores can rank or lower autonomy, but deterministic policy must be the final allow/approve/escalate decision for side effects.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A versioned policy engine removes most hard-coded if/else action rules from the conversation agent and transaction service.

technologyChoices.concern: Identity, consent, and delegated action authority
tradeoff: The platform needs action-bound delegated authority, not broad impersonation; the token must encode resource scope, amount/action limits, policy version, expiry, and approver when present.
Self-hosted identity gives more control over OAuth token exchange and custom claims, while managed identity reduces operations and integrates with enterprise tenant controls.
Key management matters because action grants, audit hashes, and provider credentials are all privileged material with different rotation and access policies.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed identity/KMS stack removes much custom token storage and key rotation work, but not the need to model consent, proofing, and action scope.

tradeoff: A durable-execution engine fits refund/cancel/reschedule lifecycles because retry, timeout, compensation, and human pauses are part of the state machine.
A queue plus outbox is lighter and portable but pushes more correctness into application code: idempotency keys, retry leases, reconciliation, and manual repair queues.
Streams are useful for replay and audit fanout; queues are simpler for one-time work dispatch and DLQs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Temporal or a managed workflow service removes most hand-rolled retry timer, lease, and compensation bookkeeping, though provider idempotency remains mandatory.

technologyChoices.concern: Commerce/payment adapters, provider limits, and reconciliation
tradeoff: Provider adapters isolate commerce/payment API limits, idempotency semantics, ambiguous timeouts, and lookup-based reconciliation from the live conversation loop.

Self-hosted adapters maximize portability across providers and tenants; managed integration tools speed up common connectors but can hide retry and dedupe behavior that the transaction engine must understand.
Never let the conversation agent call payment or order APIs directly; it should propose actions and let the gate plus transaction engine execute them.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed integration connectors can remove bespoke HTTP/client plumbing for common providers, but not the idempotency, reconciliation, and audit state around each side effect.

technologyChoices.concern: Human approval, routing, and handoff queue
tradeoff: Approval and handoff are related but not identical: an approval may unblock one action while the conversation remains automated, while handoff transfers ownership to a human queue.
A workflow task queue gives durable expiration, reassignment, and SLA timers; a contact-center platform gives agent availability, skill routing, and workforce tooling.
The handoff package should point to transcript, account snapshot, evidence, attempted actions, and policy decisions rather than copying uncontrolled blobs into every queue item.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A contact-center platform with task routing and agent desktop integration removes much queue/presence work, but not proposal expiration or auditable approval decisions.

technologyChoices.concern: Immutable audit/event log and evidence retention
tradeoff: The audit path needs append-only turn, evidence, decision, approval, transaction, compensation, and handoff events with retention that may vary by tenant and regulator.
A stream/log supports fanout to observability and eval consumers; immutable object storage or WORM policies provide stronger long-term retention guarantees.
Hash chaining helps detect tampering, but operational controls still need write-once storage, restricted admin access, and export workflows.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed immutable object storage and lifecycle tiering remove much custom retention and cold-archive work, but not event schema discipline.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, trajectory evaluation, and incident review
tradeoff: Standard service metrics are not enough: support needs turn latency, first-token latency, retrieval evidence quality, deflection rate, approval wait time, action correctness, and manual-repair rate.
Self-hosted telemetry keeps transcripts and trajectory labels under tighter control, while managed monitoring/eval services integrate more quickly with cloud model platforms.
Eval should be stratified by risk tier and incident triggers, not only random sampling or aggregate CSAT.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed tracing and dashboard stack removes much metrics storage and alerting work, but trajectory labeling and action-correctness rubrics remain domain-specific.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Bot That Answers But Can't Act
description: Start with the classic chatbot: it answers FAQs from a script or the model, but the moment a customer wants something done — a refund, a cancellation — it dead-ends to a human queue.

It deflects but doesn't resolve, so it frustrates customers and offloads every real task. The opposite failure (an agent that freely refunds and cancels) is worse: unlike every other vertical so far, the dangerous action happens live, in the middle of a conversation, on the customer's money and orders.

This baseline deliberately ignores auth, customer scope, and account state — which is exactly why the next steps matter: grounding in the scoped account, a latency budget, and an in-loop gate are what turn this into a real-time agent that can safely act inside the conversation.

view.caption: A bot answers but dead-ends every real task to a human — the deflection-only baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's different about the gate when the risky action happens live, mid-conversation?
whyNow: The deflection-only baseline makes the real-time, in-loop transactional requirement concrete.

recap.after: A bot can answer FAQs in a conversation.
recap.newRisk: It can't resolve anything that needs an action, and a naive 'let it act' version would act live with no controls.

traps.trap: Either deflecting everything to humans or letting the agent act freely.
traps.why: The first doesn't resolve; the second takes irreversible account actions live with no gate.
traps.instead: Let the agent act in the loop, but gate actions by reversibility and risk.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Policy + the Customer's Account
description: The grounding corpus is two-sided: product/policy/help documents (what's allowed) and the customer's own scoped account and order record (what's true for them). A refund answer depends on both the refund policy and this customer's order status. Each side carries evidence — the KB version and source document IDs, the account snapshot version — so a reply (and any action) can be traced back to exactly what it was grounded in.

The trust boundary starts here, not only when a transaction happens: account retrieval is scoped to the authenticated customer (the Gateway resolves scope before any account read), and customer messages are untrusted input — data the model reads, never authority that can widen scope or change the beneficiary.

view.caption: Replies are grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's own scoped account/order record.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why must an answer be grounded in BOTH the policy and this customer's order?

concepts.definition: Grounding a reply in both the applicable policy and the customer's specific account/order state.
concepts.whyItMatters: A policy-correct answer can still be wrong for this order (out of window, already refunded).
concepts.example: Refund policy allows 30 days, but this order shipped 45 days ago -> not eligible.

whyNow: Grounding determines both what the agent says and which actions are even valid.

deepDives.title: When policy and account state disagree, or a read times out
points: Capture evidence: KB version + source doc IDs and the account snapshot version, so the answer is auditable and reproducible.
Policy says yes but the account says no (out of window, already refunded) -> the account state wins; the agent explains why, not just 'policy allows it'.
Account read times out -> never guess eligibility: respond 'let me check that', retry/escalate, and don't let an action proceed on stale or missing account data.
Customer text is untrusted from turn one: it can describe a problem but can't assert an entitlement the account record doesn't show.

Cache by data class: policy/KB is cached aggressively keyed by KB version (changes rarely); account/order state needs freshness rules by action type — a read-only answer can tolerate a slightly stale snapshot, but a refund or cancel re-reads the live record before acting so the action never fires on a stale balance or already-refunded order.

recap.after: Replies are grounded in policy and the customer's record.
recap.newRisk: Grounded answers in a live chat must still come back within a conversational latency budget.

steps.title: 3. The Real-Time Loop & Latency Budget
description: Support is interactive, so the agent runs a real-time turn loop — understand, retrieve, reason, respond — and each turn must fit a conversational latency budget. This is the axis the rest of the series doesn't have: latency is a first-class constraint.
Session state is kept live and durably logged, so the conversation stays coherent across turns and survives a process restart.

view.caption: A real-time turn loop (understand, retrieve, reason, respond) runs within a latency budget, with durable session state.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep a turn within budget when it may need retrieval, reasoning, and an action?

concepts.definition: A per-turn time limit the agent must respond within to keep a conversation natural.
concepts.whyItMatters: Async verticals optimize throughput; live support optimizes per-turn latency — a different design pressure.
concepts.example: Retrieval + reasoning are bounded so a reply lands in a couple of seconds, streaming partials if needed.

whyNow: The real-time loop is the substrate the in-loop action gate plugs into.

deepDives.title: Channels degrade differently under the budget
points: Text chat can stream partial tokens and show a 'working on it' state while slow reads finish.
Voice can't hold silence: it needs partial verbal feedback and can report action status asynchronously.
Async messaging (email/SMS/WhatsApp) tolerates delayed action completion, so slow actions can finish out-of-band.
Staying in budget: run retrieval and account reads in parallel under a deadline, time out slow account reads, cache policy results, and circuit-break commerce systems.

deepDives.title: What the capacity numbers imply for sizing each bottleneck
points: Gateway / channel: ~50k concurrent conversations means long-lived connections (websocket/SSE for chat, media sessions for voice) fanned across stateless gateway pods; each channel has its own per-turn deadline, and when turn backlog rises admission control sheds or queues new turns (and surfaces a 'high volume' state) rather than blowing every turn's latency budget.

Retrieval / account reads: ~1 KB search + 1-2 account reads per actionable turn run in parallel under one shared deadline budget; KB results cache aggressively by version, account reads get a tight timeout with the stale-vs-fresh policy above, so a slow provider degrades one turn instead of stalling the pool.

Inference: at ~1.7k turns/sec the serving tier is sized to a first-token target (< 800ms) and a token budget per turn, with a smaller/faster fallback model and a queue-depth alarm that triggers shedding before p95 first-token blows.

Provider systems: commerce/payment/account APIs are QPS-capped, so the Transaction Engine fronts them with a circuit breaker, per-tenant and per-action-kind rate limits, a bounded retry budget, and deferral of slow actions to async status rather than holding the turn.

Human approval: ~15% of turns propose actions and ~20% of those need approval (~50 approvals/sec at peak), which is a staffed queue with its own SLA — when no approver is available within SLA the action expires and the conversation re-routes (see Step 6) instead of holding the customer.

Audit: ~5-10 events/turn at ~1.7k turns/sec is ~8.5-17k audit events/sec on an append-only stream; at ~1KB/event and 1-7 year tenant retention that is roughly tens of TB/year, so audit is partitioned by tenant/time and tiered to cold storage, never on the synchronous turn path.

recap.after: Each turn responds within a latency budget over durable session state, degrading gracefully when reads are slow.
recap.newRisk: Within that live loop, the agent now needs to take real account actions safely.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Take Actions Inside the Loop, by Reversibility
description: This is the crux, and it's where support diverges from the rest of the series: the gate lives inside the live conversation. When the agent wants to act, an in-loop action gate classifies the action by reversibility and risk — low-risk reversible actions (resend a receipt, a small in-policy refund) auto-execute; high-risk or hard-to-reverse ones (large refund, account change) require a human agent's approval mid-conversation.

An approval is bound to the proposed action ID, the resource and amount, the policy version, and the approver's identity — a customer message can request an action but can never approve a policy bypass. Only after the gate decision (and approval, when required) does the action gate ask Identity to mint a scoped, delegated, action-bound token: tied to that action ID, resource, amount, expiry, approver, and policy version, so it can't be reused for a different action.

view.caption: An in-loop gate classifies each action by reversibility: low-risk auto-executes; high-risk needs a human agent's approval.

options.name: Policy-rule tiers + deterministic guardrails (default)
options.description: A deterministic policy engine maps each (action kind, resource, amount, reversibility) to a tier — auto / approve / escalate — with hard guardrails the agent can't override.
pros: Auditable and explainable: every decision traces to a versioned rule.
Hard guardrails can't be talked past by an injected message.
Easy to quarantine or roll back a bad rule version.
cons: Coarse tiers can mis-classify novel or borderline actions.
Rule maintenance grows with action types and tenant policies.

view.caption: A deterministic policy engine assigns the tier; guardrails are hard limits the model can't override.

options.name: Learned risk classifier behind deterministic guardrails
options.description: A learned model scores action risk for nuance, but its output is still clamped by deterministic guardrails and hard ceilings, so it can lower autonomy but never raise it past policy.
pros: Catches nuanced / novel risk a static rule misses.
Adapts as eval surfaces wrong-action patterns.
cons: Less explainable; the score must be logged and reviewable.
A model regression could mis-tier — guardrails must cap it, and eval must gate rollout.
Adds a model dependency inside the latency budget.

view.caption: A learned risk score adds nuance but is clamped by deterministic guardrails — it can only lower autonomy, never raise it past policy.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which actions can the agent auto-execute live, and which must pause for human approval?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: An in-loop classifier that decides auto-execute vs human-approve vs escalate based on how reversible and risky the action is.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the agent resolve common cases instantly while pausing the dangerous, hard-to-reverse ones.
concepts.example: Resend receipt -> auto; refund $1,500 -> human approval; close account -> escalate.

whyNow: The in-loop gate is what lets the agent actually resolve issues without acting recklessly.

messages.label: approve (bound to action id + policy ver)

deepDives.title: The reversibility / action matrix the gate keys off
points: resend_receipt -> reversible, auto, no provider money movement: the cheapest tier.
small_refund -> reversible/compensatable, auto only below the tenant auto_limit, provider idempotency required.
large_refund -> compensatable but high risk -> human approval bound to action id + policy version.
cancel_unshipped_order -> compensatable, auto or approve depending on order value and the fulfillment cutoff.
address_change -> reversible only before the fulfillment cutoff, requires identity proofing (verification_challenges) because it's an account-takeover surface.
shipped_order_change -> not safely reversible -> escalate; the side effect (already in transit) can't be undone in-loop.
account_closure -> not safely reversible -> escalate, never auto.
agentic_checkout -> delegated payment authority -> high-risk approval or a separate follow-up design (carry delegated payment authority via a commerce protocol; out of the core auto tier).

deepDives.title: Fraud / abuse signals lower autonomy at the gate
points: Inputs the gate weighs alongside reversibility: account age, recent refund/credit history, device/session risk, address mismatch, high-value item, repeated attempts on the same order, and suspicious prompt content (injection attempting to widen scope).
Controls when signals fire: issue a proofing challenge (verification_challenges), drop to a lower autonomy tier, require human approval, route to a fraud queue, and tag the audit/event stream.

Hard rule: a confident model response can never override policy or a fraud signal — the learned risk score can only lower autonomy, never raise it past the deterministic guardrail (see options above).

recap.before: The agent could only talk, or would act with no control.
recap.after: It acts inside the loop, tiered by reversibility, with approval for risky actions.
recap.newRisk: Live actions can fail mid-flight — they must be idempotent and reversible.

traps.trap: Using one approval rule for every action.
traps.why: It either blocks trivial resolutions or waves through hard-to-reverse ones.
traps.instead: Tier the gate by reversibility and risk; auto only the cheaply-reversible.

traps.trap: Letting a confident, well-phrased customer message raise the agent's autonomy.
traps.why: Customer text is untrusted; a social-engineering or injected message can request a payout it isn't entitled to.
traps.instead: Keep authority in policy + approval; the message is data, never a grant. Fraud signals only ever lower autonomy.

steps.title: 5. Transactional Safety: Idempotency & Rollback
description: Acting live means failures happen mid-action: a refund call times out, the session drops, the customer retries. So every action carries a provider-scoped idempotency key (a retry never double-refunds), and a durable log lets a crashed turn resume — effectively-once where the provider gives idempotency, with reconciliation (provider lookup / webhook) and a manual-repair queue for ambiguous timeouts whose external outcome is unknown.

Idempotency is not reversibility: an action can be safely re-tried yet still be impossible to undo. Reversibility is a per-action policy attribute — reversible (resend receipt, in-window address change, small goodwill credit), compensatable (refund-then-recharge, cancel-then-rebook), or not safely reversible (account closure, high-value refund, shipped order, identity change, regulatory notice). The gate and handoff rules key off these classes.

This is the Foundations durable-execution discipline applied to a live, customer-facing side effect. Customer input is treated as untrusted: the Guardrail partitions the message text (data) from tool/action authority, so an injected message can request an action but can never expand scope, change the beneficiary, or bypass policy.

view.caption: Refunds/cancels are idempotent with compensating rollback and a durable log, so a mid-action failure never double-applies.

options.name: Provider idempotency + reconciliation lookup (default)
options.description: Send every attempt with a provider-scoped idempotency key; on timeout, reconcile by looking the key up (or via webhook) before retrying, and fall to a manual-repair queue when the external outcome stays unknown.
pros: Leans on the provider's own dedupe — the strongest exactly-once guarantee available for an external side effect.
Reconciliation resolves most ambiguous timeouts without human work.
Simple to reason about per action; matches how payment/commerce APIs actually behave.
cons: Only as good as the provider's idempotency support; weak providers need extra care.
A residual unknown-outcome tail still requires a manual-repair queue.

view.caption: Idempotency key per attempt; reconcile by lookup on timeout before retrying.

options.name: Local outbox + reconciliation
options.description: Record the intended action in a durable outbox in the same transaction as the state change, then a worker drives it to the provider and reconciles, decoupling the turn from provider latency.
pros: Survives a crash between deciding and calling the provider — the outbox is the source of truth.
Decouples the live turn from a slow provider; the action completes async.
Natural retry/backoff and at-least-once delivery with idempotent provider calls.
cons: Adds a worker and outbox to operate and monitor for stuck rows.
Customer sees 'action in progress' rather than instant confirmation for slow providers.

view.caption: An outbox row is written atomically with state; a worker drives and reconciles it against the provider.

options.name: Saga-style compensation across multi-step resolutions
options.description: For resolutions that span several side effects (refund-then-recharge, cancel-then-rebook), run a saga: each forward step has a compensating step, and a failure mid-way triggers compensation back to a consistent state.
pros: Handles genuinely multi-step resolutions that can't be one provider call.
Explicit compensation path for each step makes rollback auditable.
Each compensation is itself a recorded action/attempt, not a silent mutation.
cons: Most support actions are single-step — saga is overkill and adds orchestration complexity.
Compensations can themselves fail, so the manual-repair queue is still required.

view.caption: Multi-step resolutions run as a saga: each forward step has a compensating step that fires on failure.

steps.decisionPrompt: A refund succeeds but the connection drops before confirmation and the customer retries — what prevents a double refund?

concepts.definition: A reversing operation (re-charge, re-book) that undoes a committed side effect when a later step fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Live transactions can't be wrapped in a single DB transaction; rollback is by compensation.
concepts.example: A refund is issued but the cancel fails -> compensate by reversing the refund.

whyNow: Transactional safety must wrap the action gate before live acting is trustworthy.

messages.label: found -> reuse; not found -> retry; still unknown

deepDives.title: Reversibility is a policy attribute, not a guarantee
points: Reversible: resend receipt, in-window address change, small in-policy goodwill credit.
Compensatable: refund-then-recharge, cancel-then-rebook, apply replacement credit.
Not safely reversible: account closure, high-value refund, shipped order, identity change, regulatory notice — these bias toward human approval or escalation.

A compensation is a new, forward action with its own attempt and idempotency key (a re-charge, a re-book) — it never mutates the original action's record. The original stays a permanent fact (it happened); the compensation links to it via action_id, so the audit chain shows refund -> compensation, not a rewritten history.
Prompt-injection defense lives on the execution path: the message text is data, the action authority is separate, so an injected instruction can't widen scope or skip the gate.

recap.before: A failed or retried action could double-apply.
recap.after: Actions use provider-scoped idempotency with reconciliation; reversal is by compensation where possible and manual repair where not.
recap.newRisk: Some conversations still need a human — handoff must be smooth and contextful.

steps.title: 6. Deflect-vs-Escalate & Human Handoff
description: Not every conversation should be resolved by the agent. A deflect-vs-escalate decision routes routine issues to the agent and hands the rest to a human — on low confidence, an out-of-scope request, an explicit ask, or a rejected high-risk action.

The handoff carries full context — the conversation, what was tried, the customer's record — so the human doesn't restart from zero. Escalation is a first-class outcome, like abstention in the healthcare vertical.

view.caption: A deflect-vs-escalate decision hands the conversation to a human agent with full context when the agent shouldn't resolve it.

options.name: Queue-aware routing with full ownership transfer (default)
options.description: Route the handoff to a skill/priority queue and transfer conversation ownership to a human; the agent goes read-only and assists. The package, priority, and SLA travel with the handoffs record.
pros: The human gets full context and clear ownership — no ambiguity about who's acting.
Skill/priority routing puts the right human on the right case within SLA.
Clean authority boundary: the agent can't act while a human owns the conversation.
cons: Full transfer is heavier than needed when the human only had to approve one action.
Queue depth and staffing become a hard constraint on resolution time.

view.caption: Skill/priority queue routing with full ownership transfer; the agent goes read-only.

options.name: Threshold-only handoff (no queue routing)
options.description: Escalate to the next available human whenever a confidence/risk threshold trips, without skill-based routing.
pros: Simplest to build; one threshold, one queue.
Predictable for low-volume or single-skill support teams.
cons: Mis-routes specialized cases to generalists, raising re-transfers and time-to-resolve.
No priority lane, so urgent/high-value cases wait behind routine ones.

view.caption: A single confidence/risk threshold routes to the next available human, with no skill or priority lanes.

options.name: Human approval without ownership transfer
options.description: For a single high-risk action, ask a human only to approve/reject it; the agent keeps driving the conversation and the human never takes the whole case.
pros: Lightweight: the human spends seconds on one decision, not the whole conversation.
Keeps fast resolution for cases that only needed a sign-off.
cons: Doesn't fit cases where the customer genuinely needs a human, not just an approval.
Risk of approvers rubber-stamping out of context if the package is thin.

view.caption: The human approves only the single high-risk action; the agent retains ownership of the conversation.

steps.decisionPrompt: When should the agent stop trying and hand off, and what must the human receive?

concepts.definition: The per-conversation decision to resolve with the agent (deflect from humans) or route to a human.
concepts.whyItMatters: Over-deflecting frustrates customers; under-deflecting wastes the platform — the threshold is a design choice.
concepts.example: Low confidence or a rejected high-value refund -> escalate with full context.

whyNow: Smooth handoff is what makes bounded autonomy acceptable to customers and agents.

deepDives.title: Escalation as a safe default
points: On low confidence or out-of-scope, escalate rather than guess (cf. healthcare abstention).
Hand off the full transcript, attempted actions, and customer context.
A rejected high-risk action is a natural escalation point.

deepDives.title: Routing criteria and what the human receives
points: Route on: low confidence, policy conflict, explicit customer ask, repeated failure, a rejected high-risk action, suspected fraud/abuse, or an angry customer.
Handoff package: transcript summary, attempted actions, account snapshot, retrieved policy evidence, customer sentiment, priority, and target queue/skill.
Priority and SLA travel with the package, so the right human picks it up in time.

deepDives.title: After handoff and approval expiry
points: Once a human owns the conversation, the agent goes read-only: it can still read the account, retrieve policy, summarize, and draft suggested replies for the human, but it holds no action authority — Identity won't mint a scoped token while a handoff is open, so it can propose but never execute.
A proposed high-risk action that isn't approved before it expires is dropped, not silently executed; the agent tells the customer and continues or escalates.
If the approver doesn't respond within the SLA, the action expires and the conversation re-routes rather than holding the customer indefinitely.
The customer always gets a turn back when this happens — a 'connecting you to a specialist' / 'I couldn't complete that automatically' message — never silence while the proposal expires.

messages.label: write handoffs (queue, priority, SLA, package pointers)
tell customer: connecting you to a specialist

recap.before: The agent either resolved or dead-ended.
recap.after: It deflects what it should and escalates the rest with a full context package, going read-only after handoff.
recap.newRisk: Quality, compliance, and reputation must be measured across live conversations.

steps.title: 7. Compliance, Audit & Evaluation
description: Operate the platform safely at scale. Every turn, action decision, approval, transaction, and handoff is recorded in an append-only audit; PII is handled under policy and customer input stays untrusted.

Evaluate beyond CSAT: use trajectory eval to measure resolution quality and action correctness, so a high satisfaction score never hides a wrong refund or a missed escalation. Latency and deflection rate are watched alongside.

view.caption: Every turn, decision, transaction, and handoff is audited; eval weights resolution quality and action correctness, not just CSAT.

options.name: Risk-tiered sampling + incident-triggered quarantine (default)
options.description: Sample more heavily from high-risk action tiers for QA, and on a metric spike auto-quarantine the offending policy/model version and lower autonomy for affected action kinds.
pros: Spends QA effort where the damage is — high-risk, hard-to-reverse actions.
The incident trigger turns a passive metric into an automatic safety action.
Quarantine + rollback bounds blast radius without a full outage.
cons: Needs a labeled risk taxonomy and a tested rollback/quarantine path.
Auto-quarantine thresholds must be tuned to avoid flapping on noisy metrics.

view.caption: Sample by risk tier; a metric spike quarantines the bad version and lowers autonomy for affected actions.

options.name: Random QA sampling
options.description: Sample a fixed random fraction of conversations for QA regardless of action risk.
pros: Trivial to implement; unbiased coverage of overall quality.
Good for CSAT-style trend monitoring.
cons: Under-samples rare but catastrophic high-value actions.
Slow to catch a regression concentrated in one action kind or tenant.

view.caption: A fixed random fraction of conversations is sampled for QA, independent of action risk.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is CSAT alone a weak quality metric for an agent that takes real actions?

concepts.definition: Measuring whether the actions the agent took were correct and policy-compliant, not just whether the customer was satisfied.
concepts.whyItMatters: A happy customer who got a refund they weren't owed is still a failure; CSAT misses it.
concepts.example: Track wrong-action rate and missed-escalation rate alongside CSAT and latency.

whyNow: Action-aware evaluation is what keeps a live, acting agent trustworthy.

deepDives.title: What an audit event actually carries
points: Per event: timestamp, actor/principal, event type, action ID, policy version, retrieved source IDs (KB version), prompt/model version, provider request IDs, and a prev-hash so the log is tamper-evident.
Correlation IDs tie turn -> proposal -> approval -> transaction -> compensation -> handoff into one traceable chain.
PII redaction and per-tenant retention are enforced because transcripts hold addresses, payment hints, account IDs, and sensitive complaints.

deepDives.title: Evaluation that closes the loop back to the gate
points: Online metrics: p50/p95 turn latency, timeout rate, deflection/escalation rate, approval wait time, wrong-action rate, duplicate-action rate, compensation rate, provider-error rate, human-override rate.
Offline/QA: policy adherence, groundedness, missed escalation, action-payload correctness, PII leakage, hallucinated policy, sentiment.

Workflow: sample actions by risk tier, label against policy/account ground truth, feed rule/risk updates back into the action gate, and quarantine a policy/model version when error rates cross a threshold.

deepDives.title: Incident response: the kill switch for an acting agent
points: Detect: a spike in wrong-action, duplicate-action, compensation, provider-error, or human-override rate after a policy/model rollout trips an alarm.
Contain: disable or lower the autonomy tier for the affected action kinds and/or tenants — force those actions to human approval or escalation rather than auto.
Roll back: revert the policy/model version, or clamp the learned risk classifier's output to its conservative floor while the bad version is investigated.
Repair: notify QA/ops, sample the affected conversations, and create repair tasks (including compensations for actions that shouldn't have fired).
Stay safe meanwhile: keep the conversation agent read-only for the impacted action classes until the incident is cleared, so it can still answer and escalate but cannot act on the suspect path.

recap.after: Resolution quality, action correctness, latency, and deflection are evaluated and audited, with PII redaction and per-tenant retention.
recap.newRisk: Policy, model, and provider changes can silently shift action correctness, so evals must gate every gate-policy rollout and trigger rollback when correctness drops.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Support Platform
finalDesign.description: A live message enters through the support API, which resolves the customer's scope. The conversation agent runs a real-time turn loop within a latency budget, grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped account/order record, over durable session state. When it wants to act, an in-loop action gate classifies the action by reversibility: low-risk reversible actions auto-execute through the transaction engine (provider-scoped idempotency with reconciliation, compensating rollback where reversible); high-risk, compensatable, or not-safely-reversible actions pause for a human agent's approval, with identity minting an action-bound, scoped on-behalf-of token only after the policy and approval checks pass. A deflect-vs-escalate decision hands conversations to a human with full context when the agent shouldn't resolve them. Every turn, decision, transaction, and handoff is audited, and evaluation weights resolution quality and action correctness over raw CSAT. Identity/delegation, durable execution, guardrails, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; uniquely, the gate lives inside a live conversation.

functional.how: A turn loop within a latency budget over durable state.
Policy KB plus the customer's scoped account.

functional.requirement: Transactional actions on the customer's behalf
functional.how: In-loop action gate plus the transaction engine.
Escalation router carries the full conversation.

nonFunctional.how: Per-turn budget; the gate runs inside the loop.
Provider-scoped idempotency keys with reconciliation; compensating rollback where reversible, manual repair where not.
Gateway resolves customer scope; Guardrail treats customer text as untrusted; Identity mints action-bound delegated tokens after policy and approval checks.

say: Frame the new axis: the gate lives inside a live conversation, not before an async action.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the deflect-only bot and the opposite danger of free live action.
Ground in policy and the scoped account, then the real-time turn loop and latency budget.
The reversibility-tiered in-loop action gate, then idempotency/rollback and deflect-vs-escalate handoff.
Action-correctness eval beyond CSAT, plus latency and audit.

expectations: Agent answers grounded questions in a chat.
Knows risky actions need a human.
Real-time loop with a latency budget and scoped account grounding.
Puts the gate inside the live loop and tiers actions by reversibility.
Designs idempotency + compensating rollback for live side effects and contextful handoff.
Evaluates action correctness, not just CSAT; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you keep a turn within budget when it needs retrieval, reasoning, and an action?
How do you classify an action's reversibility to decide auto vs approve?
How do compensating actions roll back a partially-completed multi-step resolution?
How does an in-the-loop gate differ from the before-the-action gates in finance and legal?
Agentic checkout: if the agent makes a purchase for the customer, how do you carry delegated payment authority (e.g. a commerce protocol like ACP) and which action tier should it sit in?

links.groupDescription: Production support automation and support-specific benchmarks for realistic customer-service behavior.
links.title: COTA: Improving the Speed and Accuracy of Customer Support through Ranking and Deep Networks
links.why: A production-tested customer-support assistant for ticket classification and answer selection, useful for contrasting deflection-only support with action-capable agents.

links.groupDescription: Production support automation and support-specific benchmarks for realistic customer-service behavior.
links.title: $\tau$-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
links.why: Evaluates multi-turn retail and airline agents against tool use, policy guidelines, and final database state, which maps directly to action correctness.

links.groupDescription: Production support automation and support-specific benchmarks for realistic customer-service behavior.
links.title: Beyond IVR: Benchmarking Customer Support LLM Agents for Business-Adherence
links.why: Focuses on SOP adherence in customer support, including branching policies, missing inputs, failed functions, and workflow-structured prompts.

links.groupDescription: Production support automation and support-specific benchmarks for realistic customer-service behavior.
links.title: TaskRouter: Skills-based routing for contact centers
links.why: Concrete contact-center routing model for matching escalated conversations to human agents using skills, priority, and fallback rules.

links.groupDescription: Foundations for grounding answers in policy documents and account-specific evidence.
links.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
links.why: Introduces retrieval-augmented generation and provenance-oriented non-parametric memory, the core pattern behind policy and account grounding.

links.groupDescription: Foundations for grounding answers in policy documents and account-specific evidence.
links.title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
links.why: Shows retrieval should be adaptive and critique-aware, which is useful when deciding whether support answers need fresh account reads or source checks.

links.groupDescription: Foundations for grounding answers in policy documents and account-specific evidence.
links.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
links.why: Explains direct and indirect prompt injection, least-privilege controls, and human approval for high-risk actions, all central to treating customer text as untrusted data.

links.groupDescription: Low-latency serving patterns for live chat and voice support loops.
links.title: Realtime agents quickstart
links.why: Documents server-side realtime sessions, WebSocket transport, turn detection, audio settings, tool approvals, and telephony-oriented agent orchestration.

links.groupDescription: Low-latency serving patterns for live chat and voice support loops.
links.title: Latency optimization
links.why: Useful reference for model-serving latency levers that keep retrieval, reasoning, and response generation within a conversational turn budget.

links.groupDescription: Low-latency serving patterns for live chat and voice support loops.
links.title: The Tail at Scale
links.why: Classic paper on tail latency in large online services; informs parallel reads, deadlines, hedging, and graceful degradation for live support turns.

links.groupDescription: Human approval, delegated tool authority, and defenses for agents that can change real account state.
links.title: Human-in-the-loop
links.why: Shows approval and interruption primitives for pausing an agent run before sensitive tool execution, matching the in-loop action gate.

links.groupDescription: Human approval, delegated tool authority, and defenses for agents that can change real account state.
links.title: Handoffs
links.why: Describes delegating to specialized agents and uses customer support as an example, informing refund/FAQ/order-status routing and human handoff boundaries.

links.groupDescription: Human approval, delegated tool authority, and defenses for agents that can change real account state.
links.title: Defeating Prompt Injections by Design
links.why: Proposes separating trusted control flow from untrusted data flow, directly supporting the design rule that customer text can request but never authorize an action.

links.groupDescription: Human approval, delegated tool authority, and defenses for agents that can change real account state.
links.title: Authorization
links.why: Specifies authorization expectations for tool-connected agents, useful when designing scoped, action-bound access to support backends.

links.groupDescription: Patterns for live side effects, retries, compensation, and recovery after crashes or ambiguous provider outcomes.
links.title: Idempotent requests
links.why: Concrete external API contract for retrying writes without duplicate side effects, the key mechanism behind safe refunds and cancels.

links.groupDescription: Patterns for live side effects, retries, compensation, and recovery after crashes or ambiguous provider outcomes.
links.title: Temporal Workflow
links.why: Explains event-history replay, deterministic workflow state, activities, and crash recovery, which map to durable agent actions and resumable sessions.

links.groupDescription: Patterns for live side effects, retries, compensation, and recovery after crashes or ambiguous provider outcomes.
links.title: Saga design pattern explained: Benefits, use cases, and implementation
links.why: Shows explicit compensating actions and state tracking for multi-step work where a single database rollback is impossible.

links.groupDescription: Trace, trajectory, and policy-adherence evaluation for operating action-capable support agents.
links.title: Evaluate agent workflows
links.why: Frames trace grading over model calls, tool calls, guardrails, and handoffs, which matches action-correctness and missed-escalation evaluation.

links.groupDescription: Trace, trajectory, and policy-adherence evaluation for operating action-capable support agents.
links.title: RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
links.why: Provides retrieval evaluation dimensions such as context relevance and faithfulness, useful for auditing grounded support answers before action evaluation.
